author: bridge-bot
content: <potuz0> What's the size of the aggregated attestations for 1M validators divided by 32?

author: bridge-bot
content:
<emile17> re: **@potuz0**
> What's the size of the aggregated attestations for 1M validators divided by 32?
The stark size doesn't grow that much with the number of aggregated signatures: O(log(n).log(log(n))). What matters is really the rate of the Reed Solomon code. As a general answer I would say ≈ 256 KiB. If you are fine waiting a bit (300ms) more in the final recursive aggregation, you can even get to 128 KiB. Details can be found here (https://github.com/leanEthereum/leanVM/blob/main/misc/images/fancy-aggregation.png) (last column).

author: bridge-bot
content:
<Benewagner> re: **@emile17**
> The stark size doesn't grow that much with the number of aggregated signatures: O(log(n).log(log(n))). What matters is really the rate of the Reed Solomon code. As a general answer I would say ≈ 256 KiB. If you are fine waiting a bit (300ms) more in the final recursive aggregation, you can even get to 128 KiB. Details can be found here (last column).
That’s the size of an aggregate. If you want to divide that by 32 you get 8 KiB or 4 KiB respectively

author: bridge-bot
content:
<potuz0> re: **@GottfriedHerold**
> 32 bits or 32 bytes?
1M/32 meaning approx 31K keys. Didn't want to do the division :)

author: bridge-bot
content:
<potuz0> re: **@emile17**
> The stark size doesn't grow that much with the number of aggregated signatures: O(log(n).log(log(n))). What matters is really the rate of the Reed Solomon code. As a general answer I would say ≈ 256 KiB. If you are fine waiting a bit (300ms) more in the final recursive aggregation, you can even get to 128 KiB. Details can be found here (last column).
Thanks! So if I keep parameters as today and take about 8 different aggregates per block I'd expect 2MB blocks? Or is it guaranteed that we would have an overlapping system that will guarantee a single aggregate is enough?

author: bridge-bot
content: <emile17> you can always further aggregate your 8 previously aggregated signatures, with or without overlap.

author: bridge-bot
content:
<potuz0> re: **@emile17**
> you can always further aggregate your 8 previously aggregated signatures, with or without overlap.
Not if they are for different heada

author: bridge-bot
content: <emile17> See for instance here (https://github.com/leanEthereum/leanVM/blob/3ed1241b5e9c10d88764c2f4f453b0d5e5e4d4f4/tests/test_multisignatures.rs#L121): `single_message_a` and `single_message_b`, two previously aggregated signatures, on different messages (i.e. different heads) are aggregated together

author: bridge-bot
content: <potuz0> Ok, so just to be clear, you're telling me that the aggregation scheme is such that the proposer will be able to pack 31K attestations, evenly split among 8 different heads, with the proof to verify them in 256KBytes, is this the expectation?

author: bridge-bot
content:
<potuz0> Reason I ask is because I am trying unsuccesfully to get some feedback/immediate blocker as to why not go with the following. 

1. Attester has on-chain a PQ safe pubkey. 
2. Every epoch it generates a BLS keypair, this generation is deterministic, it takes as seed the PQ secret, an anchor root and a slot when it's going to attest. 
3. During the epoch they broadcast a message signed with the PQ key with the BLS pubkey (or a variant in which they only send the hash instead if you're worried that it can be cracked in minutes) and a ZK proof of correct derivation. The attester has minutes to generate this proof. The purpose of the proof is to ensure that the attester cannot equivocate on this message. 
4. At attestation time the attester simply signs with the provided BLS pair and anyone can aggregate. In the variant where it sent a hash it also needs to provide an opening, point being that anyone forging this attestation has to crack it in milliseconds, not seconds, not minutes. 
5. Anyone can aggregate these attestations and we do not need to rely on centralized aggregators or a new role for aggregators. 
6. Only people that have seen the broadcast in 3 can verify such aggregates. But anyone that has seen the messages can verify if the aggregate is invalid. 
7. Proposer only includes aggregations that it can verify. Because of the non-equivocation property of 3, only malicious proposers can include invalid aggregates. 
8. Attesters that have seen the message 3 can verify the aggregate/block and will only attest to blocks they can verify. Blocks with invalid aggregates can be made canonical, aggregation verification is not part of the STF. But because of the non-equivocating nature, only malicious attesters can vote for invalid aggregates. 
9. Anyone can prove that an attester/proposer is malicious by providing the original PQ signed message that had deterministic BLS key. So both proposers including <clipped message>
